So we are working until noon and then a break. And this afternoon we will have our result of homework, the looking for mind hijacks and we will have our drawing after lunch. And we will have the results of silent auction after lunch. You are having lunch, you will remember with the annoying little guy. You guys, I'll be sure to tell him I mentioned him. And so you're having lunch with him and we're reserving some time we hope at the end of the day for some more Q&A. You have dinner with Clint Arthur and I would again urge you, Clint just showed me some cool video on his old device, I would urge you not to run out of here and go eat somewhere else, let Clint feed you, spend the time with Clint, see what you can learn and then scatter as you wish because you are loose for the evening. So I am on 57 and I am starting with greed. And yes, you are coming to see me? Yes. So we will start talking about greed. And here is I think what people may not understand about it. So for example, a lot of people think you can't motivate rich people with greed because they are already rich. Wall Street crime would tell us differently. I would say if you put 10 billionaires in a room and you ask for a show of hands of how many of them need more money and you use the word need, at least nine out of ten hands are going up. And something like the Martha Stewart case is profound evidence. I mean here is somebody who needed to know, what are you doing? Oh, do we have a problem over there? So here is Martha Stewart at the time certainly with no sensible need for money who engages in what she had to know because her own company is a public company, was illegal insider trading to prevent a loss on a stock that is equivalent to pocket change in her world. And of course, ultimately there was no underlying crime. It's always a surprise to people that you can actually go to jail for the obstruction of the investigation even when there is no underlying crime. We can only hope. I think it would probably take 15 years off my life. I think I would just get younger the minute I saw it. So first of all, the Martha Stewart case is classic and is an aside. What I learned with the client in the theft control business is this. Five percent of the population is hardwired so that they cannot lie cheat or steal. They are the Jim Carrey character in the movie who just could not do anything but tell the truth. We can pity them by the way. They are good if you can hire them as long as they don't talk to your customers. You don't want one of these people talking to your customers. But they absolutely cannot. She asked, does this make me look fat? He's saying yes. And starving, family starving, loaf of bread bounces off a bread truck. She's picking it up and chasing the truck for eight blocks to give the loaf of bread back. There's five percent of the population. That is this way. Most of you will find that hard to believe because I doubt any of you are in that five percent. Maybe Nelson. Maybe. Maybe. Then there's five percent of the population that is hardwired exactly the opposite way. They must lie cheat, steal all the time even when it does not serve their purposes whatsoever. So they could be a self-made billionaire, entrepreneur, and they would lie about their income. I mean, that sort of thing. They just cannot help themselves. Now that takes care of 10 percent of the population. The rest of it is all situational, which is where we are. And we require three basic things in order to be greedy, in order to want something for nothing, in order to steal. We need three things. We need perceived need. And if you are managing people, by the way, no, you can never control this. We can't control what goes on in somebody's head. We need the ability to rationalize the behavior, which is if you have anybody around you who seems to have no ability whatsoever, I promise you that you will have this ability. So the ability to rationalize the behavior. So I can employ you theft. This is I'm doing all the work. They're getting all the money. You just got a new car. You're taking a big vacation. I can't take a vacation. Yada, yada, yada. Boom. I can take a little. I deserve it. Employee theft, by the way, interesting stat for you as an aside, employee theft in the last seven years is up almost 50%. You ought to worry about it if you have a budget of employees. It is my contention that that rise is directly related to societal, media, and political furnishing of rationalization encouragement. Right? And that's driving it. So that's the second thing. The third thing is the belief you can go undetected. If those three things are present, hence Martha, then greed in general and in specific theft will occur with 90% of the population. And in management, by the way, the only thing you can control is the third. The only thing you can exercise any control over is whether or not they believe they can engage in the behavior undetected. Right? And so, and it isn't just stealing stuff. So it's like Jay's whole business. Did he stay in hiding this morning? So Jay's whole business is really all about theft control. The phone part of it, because when you have a protocol in your office for how the phone is supposed to be answered most effectively and they violate that protocol, they're stealing from you. That's what they're doing. And the only way to stop them is if they're afraid they're going to get caught, which means they got to know they're being mystery shopped all the time. I mean, it's, you know, so in the broad sense, greed is visceral. It is not really for really 95% of us, all of us who are situational, plus the five. It is not really optional. It's not really consciously managed. It's visceral. It's like a full dog will still eat a hunk of steak on the floor. That's what dogs do. It's visceral. Therefore, it is stimulatable in just about anybody. So I mentioned Bob Stupak yesterday. It's one of the great basic examples. So Bob built Vegas World from the one-story thing to a giant hotel and then two towers and ultimately he became a stratosphere. He built it with virtually no financing, all self-generated money by selling prepaid vacation packages. And they were sold in print and he'd have a field day with the internet if he was alive. They were sold in print and they were sold by direct mail. And so a list of people who would have a higher than average likelihood to buy a vacation package to Vegas is gold, right? So how do you get that list? So when you checked into Vegas World, you got this bag of goodies. You got freebies and you got stuff and you got a hat and you got whatever. You got, sometimes it was three, most of the time it was five picture postcards like you would buy downstairs in the gift shop on the rack. One of them had a big picture of the casino floor and another one, the million-dollar stacked up that you could go get your picture in front of if you wanted to and so forth. And so there's these picture postcards and they had a toilet strip around them, like around the toilet seat in the Motel 6. And it said, don't put any stamps on these. We'll mail them for you. Just put them in the special mail slot downstairs and we're happy to send them home to your family and friends for you. So think about this. It's irresistible. By not doing it, you're wasting money. So everybody, everybody, if they had to call home to get an address, if they had to, whatever, the redemption was nearly 100%. So basically every two days, 300 rooms, times three is 900 leads, times five is 1500 leads, times 150 days a year is 100,000 names. I mean, it's a lot of leads. So manually, of course, before these things got mailed, somebody had the data entry, all the names and addresses, which is a relatively cheap way. As Bob said, there is no cheaper way to get this list. And now timing. So we mail their postcard today and a week later we mail them the Pitch Package for the Vegas World Vacation that their friends were just on. And by the way, what does everybody say right on these postcards? Regardless of the reality. Right? What are they right? Man, we're having a great time. This is a cool place. Right? Hardly anybody writes on this. This sucks. Right? Right? Well, because when you go somewhere, you want to lord it over your family and friends. You don't want to confess to being an idiot. Right? So I mean, you know, if you go to the Bahamas and there's a hurricane and you're locked in your hotel the entire time in fear of your life, you never admit it when you go home. It's, oh, it's great. We're on a beach every day. Right? So, you know, Stupak's place had its flaws. Plus, you lose money. But all the postcards are, man, we're having a great time. I won $180 tonight. The show was terrific. Boom. Here comes the vacation package. This whole thing is driven by greed because I won't not use my five free postage paid postcards. Right? By the way, that little trick can work in a lot of businesses. I've taught it for 20 some odd years and hardly anybody ever uses it. But a sort of a version of it now is Facebook. Right? Is you'll be here and you'll post to Facebook what a great time you're having and so forth. It's still not as effective, by the way, as if I had you mail five postcards. But so this is a very pushable thing. I fought whether poor, rich, smart, dumb, highly educated, uneducated. It almost doesn't matter. So a pure greed pitch for you. Is there anybody in here who's in the true, you know, get rich in real estate business? Like, well, David, you're probably closest, Phelps. But you... Not... Yeah. I mean, not quite. So if you take around a grand audience or a kiyosaki audience or the guy on one of the ones that has a reality to flip this house or something on TV and they build a seminar tour around him. Right? And so they'll run big newspaper ads. They'll be on TV. They'll be on radio and they'll fill free preview seminars like here. Right? Like Trump University. Same business. Right? And one of the criteria for choosing your hotels, by the way, in that business is that they're within walking distance of a bus stop. I'm not kidding. So if you watch, if you go to one, you will see two-thirds of the room are kind of what you would expect from a...you could get rich in real estate with no brains, no credit, no experience, no money, doing no work in 72 days or less. Right? Two-thirds of that room is exactly what you would expect that to produce. Right? And they came by bus from the trailer park. Mom ain't with dad because when they go out and they share one set of teeth and they can't go out and pop...they can't go out in public together. It's an old Charlie Jarvis joke if you want to credit. And so it's exactly what you would expect. Right? It's instructive, by the way. If you see one of these things advertised, you have to sit through the seminar, just go out and walk outside the room and watch everybody who arrives. It's interesting. And so two-thirds of the room, most places, is exactly what you would expect. However, the other third of the room is what's really instructive because they are not them at all. They are doctors, lawyers, CPAs, executives, retired executives, and it is like two different groups of people arriving at the same place. Each shocked, by the way, somewhat to see the other. Right? And how can that be? Well, because doctors, lawyers, they're all susceptible to a greed pitch. They all are. And so I didn't bring it this time, but I have an ad that is a really dumb get-rich-in-the-stock-market ad. I mean, it is...if you have just any discernment at all, you would recoil from this ad because it is the equivalent of the worst of the get-rich-in-real estate. Right? It is...you don't have to know anything about the stock market. We're going to flash a red light when you sell and a green light when you buy, and it takes 10 minutes a day. And if you don't have a computer, you can use a broker and do it on the phone, and you're going to get just wildly rich. And here's my last $10 million trades and all the shit we've talked about by the way. I mean, it's a magnificent ad, but it is so blatantly obvious that you would think people would recoil from it. And the ad has...over the years, it's been very successful in just newspapers, remnant buys in newspapers. Oh, okay. You say, all right, I get that, right? Because there's a lot of the first group I just described who reads the newspaper. Right? However, it works equally well in investors' business daily, which is a microcosm of the Wall Street Journal reader. Investors...almost all investors' business daily subscribers also subscribe to the Wall Street Journal. They just can't get enough 8-point statistics just from the Wall Street Journal, so they need investors' business daily too. All right? It would work in the Wall Street Journal. It's just that the Wall Street Journal won't run it, but investors' business daily will. So it's a...and it's a blatant greed pitch. And there is a percentage of every population who can't resist it because it's visceral. All right? And so the car business...the car industry gets away with selling cars that are discounted by $8,000 this weekend. Now, if you have a brain the size of a flea, you know this is horse shit. Right? I mean, you may not know how, but you know this is horse shit. But it's not just stupid people that go buy cars on the weekend that they're giving an $8,000 discount on a free television. People who should know better go too because it's getting them here. All right? It's getting them here. And so you want to use it even when you think you shouldn't. Now, I...in most cases you don't want to rely on it and you have to...it's dynamite. You have to be careful with it, but free is still magic. Free is one of those things roughly every few years just like with long copy. There's a whole little tribe of people who surface in all the advertising trade journals. So if you read Ad Age and Target and DM News and all that stuff, there will be an outburst of declaring free dead. It happens like every three to five years. There's a big year-long funeral for the word free, right? That...and they will contend that everybody has wised up. And finally, the consumer has access to so much...the latest funeral, you know, has been based on the web. But now the consumer has access to so much information. And they're so much smarter that free is dead. This has been going on for as long as I've been alive. They keep burying it and it keeps crawling back out of the grave and working just like long copy. So there's the funeral for long copy. It's held every few years. Everybody in the advertising business goes. It's a big to-do. They bury it. And as soon as they walk away, it comes back out of the grave and, you know, reappears. Say...save thing with free. Because you...people have a unconscious, literally hardwired in, visceral desire for it that often is irresistible, even when on a conscious and intellectual level, they know it's nonsense. Similarly, new works. I'm on your page 58. New works because of our fatigue with the old. And so this example from an old book, 30-year-old book, The Joyless Economy, is really a good example. And we all understand it. All right? So you get a new car. Even if it's not brand new, new to you, but mostly a new car. On average, you have shopped for at least three weeks, three weekends, before you have got it. So you've worked up a fair amount of anticipation for how happy you are going to be with this new car. And you got it now. And you're pretty happy for a few days. But as the new car smell is wearing off, and you never saw anybody else driving it before, and now at every traffic light, there's somebody next to you with the same damn car, and you discover that in a parking lot, you can't find it because you can't tell it apart from 16 other kinds of cars. So like if you bought a new, how do they keep saying it in the commercial? You don't say Jaguar. What do you say? Say it again? Yeah, a Jaguar. Right? So if you've bought yourself a new Jaguar, and you're real happy with it, your happiness will start to descend the moment you're parked next to a Ford Taurus. Because first of all, it's the same damn car, in case you didn't know it. It's on a Taurus frame. It's got Taurus parts. It's a Taurus. And it's five times the cost, right? The other thing that starts to happen is all the little shit you didn't notice when you test drove the car for 10 minutes with the sales guy next to you talking, you begin to notice it. Somebody, I had a loaner. What did I have? I had a Bentley from my car guy. Here's where the cup holder is. And I'll bet you most people, when they test drive the Bentley, they never notice. Right? Because you're infatuated with the dashboard and the gadgets and the guys talking and you're driving a Bentley for Christ's sake, and you didn't get in the car with a cup. The cup holder is here. Here. To get the cup. You got to either do this or you got to do this. I'm cussing this thing. I mean, I go from my house to the convenience store, I get a cup of coffee and I'm cussing it in five minutes. I'm thinking, if I paid 300 grand for this thing, I would really be pissed. Right? Because you're going to drive like this from now on. You know? Really? So you start to notice all the little shit, you know, the buttons in the wrong place, you know, all that. It didn't very long before your pleasure with the new is gone. Right? And so fatigue with the old, even though it may work, it may be good. It may be comfortable. So Procter and Gamble figured it out in, I think, the 60s. I think this dates to the early 1960s. And they haven't deviated from its sense, by the way. I saw the latest one on TV the other day. So Procter and Gamble at one point had tied. That's what they had. There were like five detergents. Each company had one. Procter and Gamble had tied. Lever Brothers had some. I mean, there were like five. Right? And Tide works fine. Now, remember, we're in the 60s. So Mama still is, in most cases, a homemaker. She is at home. This is her job. So Tide worked fine. Their own consumer surveys. Nobody stopped using Tide because it wasn't cleaning the clothes. They switched just because they were bored. They bought a different detergent after using Tide for a month or two months or three months just because they were sick of seeing the damn box. Just because it was the same old stuff. Now this is really important about new. And that is, it doesn't matter necessarily how well what you are delivering works. They will still grow unhappy with it. And by the way, for those of you in the information marketing business, piling more of the same on won't make them any happier. As they are losing the pleasure that comes with new, more of old will not counteract that emotional reaction. Truth be told, you would probably be as well served this weekend if we had just repeated the last year's seminar. I can't sell it. Nobody would be here. But truth be told, you would be as well served. So what Procter and Gamble did, the first thing they did is they quickly created a bunch of different detergents, all their own. Idea being, if Mary's going to buy Tide three times and then she's going to buy something else, let's try and make the something else tied in a different box. They weren't really subtle. The first one was called surf. And it tied as an orange box, a blue circle in the middle of the target. Surf, maybe still does, had a green box with an orange thing in the circle and instead of Tide, it said surf. I mean, they didn't stay up late. And it's tied in the box. It's the same stuff, right? Then they did another one, then they did another one, then they did another one. And the second thing they did, because Tide was still the mainstay and still is today. Tide I think accounts for just about 15% of the total detergent sales in America, which is a big number. So then the next thing they did is they created the new and improved cycle. So, Trump has a new cycle, right? It's every two to three days. That's his whole, that's his gig, right? And that's why he doesn't care about really putting his foot in it. Because he's going to change the new cycle anyway. And they're all going to talk about something else. So it doesn't matter what he does really, as long as they'll let him keep changing the new cycle, right? Oh, John McCain's not a hero. I only like people that don't get shot down. Oh, Christ. Really? Did you just say that? Yeah? Carl O'Leary looks at me and says, that ought to do it. I said, ain't going to matter, right? Because three days from now we're going to be on something else. So Tide created a new and improved cycle, right? Which is every six weeks. And it has been every six weeks since they started this. They do something. They sent it. They take the scent out of it. They put blue sprinkles in that purportedly do something. They take the blue sprinkles out. They make it in a box with a pore spout. They put it in a bottle. They put it in a squeegee deal. The latest thing they've been doing, which is really comical. And I know everybody falls for it. We fall for it. I can see it on the shelf and I have. Is the booster product. This is hilarious. I understand.
